sentences.text: That's correct.
No, that was in the beginning, [GERMAN], which means that was a-- a camp where people were compelled to work and stay.
Later on, it was renamed to koncentrationslager, which means stricter, much stricter than in the normal lager conditions.
It was a rigor, a time for everything.
You had to comply with these things.
Otherwise, you were punished.
So far, men were separated from women.
But we were allowed to contact after hours, after work.
Very bad.
Very bad.
You had very little space for you to sit or lie or keep your goods, whatever you wanted.
It was different in Plaszow than it was in ghetto.
Oh yes.
I know exactly, about 20,000 people.
And there were some.
Outside the living quarters, there were some workshops or workplaces.
They were different according to people who are giving their occupation.
There was tailor, [INAUDIBLE], machine specialist, [GERMAN], which means boot repairers, even brush manufacturing.
And they were two, also, big firms, like [INAUDIBLE] who worked for the army.
We received some orders to supply.
There was a-- quite an organization administration in the camp.
Oh, we used to go out to work in Cracow.
Of course, in groups, always surrounded by Ukrainians or German people, German soldiers, in different areas of Cracow.
The food was distributed by barracks of officials.
They were always people-- they called it barrack alteste, or the oldest one.
Sometimes it was a man.
Sometimes it was a woman.
And all these things were rationed according to the amount of people.
Every morning, we had appells.
We had to count all persons who are in the sick rooms in the hospital, or he couldn't stay on the appellplatz.
It was in the-- in the hands of Jews, yes.
Oh, yes.
Sometimes they were-- people nominated who were known to the administration that the people suitable for that sort of things.
And sometimes they were people who volunteered for it, who felt that they can cope with the-- more than 20, 30, or 40 people.
Hmm, it's hard for me to tell you.
I would guess that sometimes it was up to-- up to 50.
Oh, there were common washrooms, common latrines, or conveniences, as we say, toilets.
There was water available, not in a great way.
And everything was always in a hurry.
Oh, they were people specially chosen to do that.
Yes.
Oh, no.
We had, by that time, different ways.
First of all, they were painted us with paint.
We used to work in-- in camps with special uniforms.
In the beginning, we used to have our private suits.
But, later on, they wanted to unify this.
And we had to dispose of our private things and have only that what was necessary.
We couldn't carry much, only that what we are clothes.
Because we had to have free hands and legs for work.
In Plaszow, we had a different situation.
There was a Polish smaller camp inside.
And outside were-- was the Jewish camp.
But we were all surrounded by Ukrainian and German police-- police.
In our camp, I don't remember women, no.
First time I know about that was in Auschwitz, or other camps.
Oh, they were, in the beginning, very sympathetic.
Because nobody likes to be surrounded by the-- nobody could do what you wanted.
You had to do what you are ordered.
But, later on, they were probably ignoring us.
In Plaszow, we stayed-- I stayed till the 15th of October, 1944.
That was the time when Schindler organized a factory in Czechoslovakia.
And he needed 700 men.
And he was allowed also, to take 300 women.
But if you're asking about that, I have to go a little bit back and say, before we came all into Plaszow area, we used to live in the areas outside Cracow.
My mother had a small house, 24 kilometers behind Cracow.
And when we were not allowed to live in Cracow, I sent my mother, with my sister and her husband, into this small house 24 kilometers-- Niepolomice was the name of the place.
A village, I would say.
Later on, when the-- when the German organized transport to that sort of camps, like Treblinka, Belzec, and so on, I brought my mother back to Cracow.
And I had the chance to keep her in our non-Jewish place.
But my brothers, who were also with her, unfortunately, they had to go with the transport.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the transport stopped in the forest for a change of signals.
They escaped.
The transport wasn't very much surrounded.
And that was the first thing, what my brothers had done, to ring me back.
I was working still, in the company.
And they ask what to do.
So we told them, we know what to do.
Come back to Cracow.
By that time, Schindler organized a group of 200 young men for work in-- in the-- in [NON-ENGLISH].
That was a place behind the ghetto, but close to ghetto.
Later on, all these people had a chance to put their families on this so-called Schindler's List.
So my two brothers put on the list myself, my wife, my mother, my sister, and brother-in-law.
So on the 15th of October, as I mentioned, 700 men was put into wagons.
And we went to Gross Rosen, or Rosen.
What year is that?
Oh, there were different forces.
Usually, there was the administration.
Then, there were the police, Jewish police.
And according to the occupation or adaptability of people, they were put on the so-called Schindler's List.
No.
No.
Not at all.
No.
In the ghetto, yes, we heard.
But in Plaszow, all these ties were broken.
They were.
They were.
I remember it was something done.
But I was-- didn't participate-- participate in that sort of things.
And I can't give you any report about it.
But I am sure it was.
There were groups who were observant, who knew exactly what holiday's approaching.
They kept a calendar.
I did not.
Yes.
Yeah.
There were different reasons, sometimes political, sometimes-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --they kept it-- I was told, I haven't been there-- it was a brothel.
They brought some girls from Cracow, when they were-- they are needed for the Ukrainian--
--or even for German.
There were, of course, reasons why they kept them.
But we didn't, in the beginning, mix up with them.
But, later on, there was a mixture.
I was working in the building department with a group of Polish people.
And I was commanding them.
We went outside ghetto, through the neighborhood.
We had to clean some houses for German officials, for the [INAUDIBLE], for the director of rebuilding.
And we were allowed to come back into the area where is the kitchen to collect food, soup for them.
But, sometimes, there were Jewish groups.
I remember when a big group who was organized by Mr.
Rosner.
R-O-S-N-E-R.
That was closed-- that was called Luftgau-Kommando.
That was a place where only air-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] airmen were off-- off-- officers of the air fleet were having their casino.
And there was time that we were allowed to work.
I worked in the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --group who was cleaning the places, removing furniture, and helping in the kitchen to bring in coal, vegetable, and so on.
So we had a little bit better food than others.
They were.
But I can't give you exactly, a report.
There were sometimes people who are cleaning offices, repairing some furniture, repairing some-- a good [? deal ?] of some broken glass in-- or even carpenter.
They were.
Riskier?
No.
We were always with somebody who was watching us.
So we couldn't do anything risky.
Yes.
Yes.
We went to Gross Rosen.
It was, I think, one or two days' train.
By-- by that time, we were together, my two brothers and my brother-in-law.
We were always kept together.
Oh, they were-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --just ordinary wagons of carrying, sometimes, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] material.
But quite often, it was used only for transport to the camps.
No.
By that time, it was a very short travel.
No.
We used to have some food, which we received before we were put into the wagons.
But later on, I don't remember that we received nothing.
It was-- it was-- we were closed.
They were shut.
And maybe there were some locks on it or hooks.
No.
It was nine-- oh, that was October or November, yes.
It was already, in-- in the Polish climate, it was beginning of winter or rather, late of autumn.
Oh, very, very-- that was a short travel.
I think 2-3 days.
I can't recollect it at the moment.
Oh, there were three or four days.
First, we had to remove all our private clothes.
We had to go to the washrooms.
They were shaving us, the hair.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were put on a floor covered by straw.
Like sardines, we were put in together.
The morning was an appell.
And we knew that we will have be, sooner or later, transported to Czechoslovakia.
By that time, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I think that we received, in Gross Rosen, a new clothes, stripes.
And the number was sewn on our shirt, or on our blouse.
Yes.
We had also, a cover of our head, a sort of a cap.
It was no work whatsoever.
It was all the time appell, counting and counting, and standing and waiting for the next time when we would be called to take soup.
I heard they were.
But we were a separate group, which was always separated from the others.
We didn't have any contacts, no.
Well, there was probably from Gross Rosen, the staff.
Transit camp, that's right.
No, women stayed in Plaszow.
And they had to go two weeks after.
But our contact was broken.
We did not know anything, what's happening.
Only after a few months, when woman came to Brinnlitz, I found that my mother was holding hands with-- hands with my sister.
And I was surprised not to see my wife.
Then, I was informed that my wife was crossed out from the list by somebody.
And I don't know, to this day, who has done it.
I was married on the 21st of March, 1941.
And that was, already there, '44.
So we were separated, of course.
In Cracow, in the ghetto.
The name?
The name was Felicity from [? home, ?] [?
Greenbaum. ?] And married name, Gutherz.
Her name was G-U-T-H-E-R-Z.
Oh, yes, it was.
We had to go into ghetto, into rabbi's place.
And it was a ritual, Jewish.
I have still a certificate, a copy of the certificate of marriage, which I arranged after we were repatriated to Poland.
Yes.
Which means not in the same barrack.
Men were separated, women separated.
But we had a chance to see each other, especially that my wife was chosen to work in the kitchen, in the administration.
She was accepting the numbers from every barrack, how many soups we are receiving for each barrack.
So we had a contact to see her.
No.

When my wife was not called when the 300 women were assembled to go, she decided to keep with her brother, with her sister, and sister-in-law, together, up to the end of existing, until the 15th of January, 1945.
She was there up to the liquidation with her brother, sister, sister-in-law.
In Plaszow.
And they went, on foot, to Auschwitz.
From Gross Rosen, directly, we went to Brinnlitz, Bratislava, in Moravia.
It's part of the Czechoslovakia.
Oh, it was several days.
Only with the same wagons which we received in Cracow-Plaszow.
Very bad.
It's hard to describe this.
Because we were nearly 50 people or more sometimes, in the wagons.
You couldn't always sit.
You couldn't always lie.
You couldn't always stay.
But we survived.
No.
There was a dish where everybody was using for that sort of purpose.
And when there were stoppages, they were emptying the dish.
Not to my knowledge during the travel from Gross Rosen.
Only, we received some food in Gross Rosen, because it was anticipated that it will take longer.
But I don't remember that somebody starved because of that, no.
Yes.
I don't remember any outsiders.
Maybe they were not born in Cracow.
But maybe they came from the country area surrounding-- neighboring with Cracow.
But we were, more or less, amalgamated into the same society.
Oh, no.
My father was, during the outbreak of the war, in Warsaw, between the 1st and 10th September, 1939, when Warsaw was bombard heavily.
After that, we were trying to contact him.
And all trace was missing.
We tried to find him through the Red Cross in Geneva.
And they were not finding him.
No.
Not at all.
He was missing from the first day of the war.
We were always only with our-- our mother.
Oh, yes.
In a separate barrack, yes.
Together with my sister.
Yes.
We were always keeping together.
We are never separating.
It was possible.
It wasn't difficult.
Yeah, yes.
It was called concentration camp because we were directly responsible to Gross Rosen.
But strictly speaking, every order came from Berlin.
We had the staff from Germany.
All-- all-- all of them were soldiers, one or two probably, they were part of the SS.
Yes.
We noticed that Schindler was standing on the ramp, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] welcomed us with spread arms, and promised that if we follow all his instructions, we will have a chance to survive.
700 Men.
Hmm.
It was extraordinary, how they call him a person, always smiling, always having his cigar or cigarette.
He was a heavy smoker.
But always, very friendly, listening to everybody, and helpful.
No.
He was in his civilian things, with his Tyrolean hat, a very well-known personality, and very loved by everybody.
By that time, when I was 5-- 27, 28 or 30, he was probably 40 or 45, a well-built person, a very friendly person.
Even if he had to belong to the-- unless they are paid, I mean, to the Nazi group, because that was an order.
If he wanted to go with his ideas, he had to belong to the party.
But he was a great sympathizer.
And he did not believe for one moment that-- that Germany will win the war.
From the beginning, he belonged to that sort of well-orientated people that Germany will lose the war.
Always in German, in plain German language.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Many of them, probably because there are some similarities between Jewish jargon.
But many people understood because they were studying the language as a additional language to the Polish.
Some people had a chance to pick up French language.
But I picked German.
No.
No.
I can't remember that was an order.
Because it was self-- If you didn't understand and you didn't follow the order, you were hit or shot.
Good.
